title: Baby Milk & Formula Calculator | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much Breast Milk or Formula Does Your Baby Need?
description: 🩺 Baby formula calculator: Find how many ml your baby needs per feeding by age and weight. Based on WHO & AAP guidelines. Quick and free.
intro: Knowing **how much milk** your baby needs is one of the most common questions in the first months. The amount varies by **age**, **weight**, and whether your baby is **breastfeeding or formula feeding**. This calculator gives you an estimate based on **WHO** and **AAP (American Academy of Pediatrics)** guidelines.
keyTakeaway: A newborn drinks **30-60 ml** per feeding. By 3 months, it increases to **120-150 ml**. The general rule for formula is **150 ml/kg/day** divided among feedings.
useCases: Your newborn just arrived and you want to know how many ml to give per feeding.
You're combining breastfeeding and formula and need to calculate the right amounts.
Your pediatrician asked you to keep a feeding log.
You're starting to pump and want to know how much milk you should be producing.
You want to know if your baby is eating enough.

dataUpdate.notes: WHO and AAP recommendations for infant feeding.

example.title: Example: 3-month-old baby, 5.5 kg, formula feeding
example.result: Your baby needs ~**825 ml/day** of formula, distributed across **~6 feedings** of **~140 ml** each.

explanation:
## Milk Intake by Age

| Age | ml per feeding | Feedings/day | Daily total |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0-2 weeks | 30-60 ml | 8-12 | 300-600 ml |
| 2-4 weeks | 60-90 ml | 8-10 | 500-700 ml |
| 1-2 months | 90-120 ml | 7-9 | 600-900 ml |
| 2-3 months | 120-150 ml | 6-8 | 750-950 ml |
| 3-4 months | 150-180 ml | 6-7 | 800-1000 ml |
| 4-6 months | 150-210 ml | 5-6 | 800-1000 ml |
| 6-9 months | 180-240 ml | 4-5 | 600-800 ml + solids |
| 9-12 months | 180-240 ml | 3-4 | 500-600 ml + solids |

## Formula Feeding Rule

```
ml/day = weight (kg) × 150
```

This applies from 1 month to 4-5 months of age. After that, solid foods are introduced and milk intake decreases.

## Breastfeeding: How to Know if Baby is Getting Enough

With exclusive breastfeeding, you can't measure ml, but these signs show your baby is eating well:
- **6-8 wet diapers** per day
- **Steady weight gain** at pediatric checkups
- **Baby seems satisfied** after feedings
- **3-4 bowel movements** daily (first month)

## Related Calculators

- [Breast Pump Calculator](/calculadora-sacaleches-produccion) — milk supply and pumping output.
- [Breastfeeding Calories](/calculadora-calorias-lactancia) — extra calories needed while nursing.
- [Baby Growth Percentile](/calculadora-percentil-bebe-oms) — growth charts and milestones.

faq.q: How much milk should a newborn drink per feeding?
faq.a: A newborn typically drinks **30-60 ml** per feeding during the first 2 weeks, with **8-12 feedings** per day. A newborn's stomach is the size of a cherry (5 ml) at birth, but grows quickly over the first weeks.

faq.q: How do I know if my breastfed baby is getting enough milk?
faq.a: Signs of good breastfeeding: **6-8 wet diapers per day**, **steady weight gain** at pediatric visits, baby **seems satisfied** after feeding, and at least **2-3 stools** daily in the first month.

faq.q: Does the 150 ml/kg rule work for all babies?
faq.a: It's a general guideline for **formula feeding**. Some babies need more (up to 180 ml/kg) and others less. What matters most is that your baby **gains weight steadily** and seems satisfied. Your pediatrician will adjust based on your baby's needs.

faq.q: At what age does baby need less milk?
faq.a: Starting at **6 months**, solid foods are introduced and milk intake gradually decreases. By 12 months, milk is around **500-600 ml/day** and solid foods become the main source of nutrition.

faq.q: Can you overfeed a baby with a bottle?
faq.a: Yes, it's easier to overfeed with a bottle than breastfeeding. Formula flows faster and babies may drink more than they need. Use a **slow-flow nipple**, take feeding breaks, and watch for hunger/fullness cues (turning head, closing mouth).

faq.q: Does breast milk and formula have the same calories?
faq.a: Roughly yes: both have about **67 kcal/100 ml**. The main difference is that breast milk contains antibodies, enzymes, and growth factors that formula can't replicate.

faq.q: When should I stop giving formula?
faq.a: WHO recommends breast milk until **2 years or beyond**. Stage 1 infant formula is used until 6 months, then Stage 2 follow-up formula until 12 months. After that, whole cow's milk can be introduced.

faq.q: How many ml should my 3-month-old drink per day?
faq.a: A 3-month-old typically needs **600-900 ml per day** in 6-7 feedings, or about **120-150 ml per feeding**. Use the calculator above to get a personalized estimate based on your baby's age and weight.

sources.name: WHO — Infant and Young Child Feeding
